content.text: A literal type is the type of one specific value — not "any string" but exactly "north", not "any number" but exactly 404. Literal types let you constrain a value to a fixed set of options with full type safety and IDE autocompletion.

attrs.alt: Literal types are exact single-value subsets of their base types (string, number, boolean), combined into unions to form constrained sets of allowed values

content.text: A string literal type restricts a variable to a single string value. Combined into a union, they form a fixed set of allowed strings — the most common use case in Angular code.
Template literal types (TypeScript 4.1+) compose literal types using string interpolation syntax, generating the cross-product of their member types.
Number literal types restrict a value to specific integers or floats. They are most useful for HTTP status codes, port numbers, version constants, and numeric state machines.
true and false are literal types in TypeScript. Combined, they form boolean. On their own they allow functions to signal state through their return type alone.
By default, TypeScript widens object and array literals to their base types. The as const assertion tells TypeScript to keep the most specific literal type for every value in the expression.
Comparing const, as const, and Type Annotation
Literal types in overload signatures allow TypeScript to track which specific return type matches which specific input.

When a variable holds a union of literals, TypeScript narrows the type inside each branch of a conditional so that only the matching literal is active.
Literal Types in Mapped Types and Utility Types
Literal types combine naturally with mapped types to generate property sets or transform keys.
Literal types make test assertions more precise. They also enable mock factories that return correctly-typed test doubles without casting.
Literal types pin a type to a specific value, enabling constrained APIs with full compile-time safety:
— restrict a string to an exact set of values; preferred over enums in modern Angular code
— restrict to specific numeric values; useful for HTTP codes, port numbers, step indices
— each is its own literal type; used as discriminants in tagged unions (
